questions.question: What is distinctive about the flagship Cloud Load Balancing (Application Load Balancer) on Google Cloud compared with a typical regional LB?
options: It is a single global anycast front end — one IP routes each user to the nearest healthy backend, so geo-steering happens at the LB, not at DNS
It requires a separate GeoDNS service to steer users to the closest region before traffic reaches the LB
It is always regional and you must deploy one LB per region with DNS round-robin across them
It only balances raw TCP/UDP at L4 and cannot route HTTP(S) by path or host
questions.explanation: GCP's global Application Load Balancer is a single anycast IP that routes to the nearest healthy region, collapsing 'GeoDNS + regional LB' into one front end. The DNS-based steering and per-region options describe other clouds' patterns, and the L4-only option describes the Network LB.

questions.question: What is the recommended way to let a Cloud Run service authenticate to other Google Cloud services without managing keys?
options: Attach a service account to the compute; it receives short-lived credentials automatically (Workload Identity on GKE)
Download a service-account key JSON and bake it into the container image
Store a long-lived API key in an environment variable on the service
Embed the project owner's user password in Secret Manager and read it at startup
questions.explanation: Attaching a service account gives the workload short-lived credentials with no key files (Workload Identity on GKE). A downloaded key JSON in the repo is an explicit red flag, and secrets/passwords in env vars are exactly the anti-patterns the reviewer warns against.

questions.question: Which Cloud Storage feature automatically transitions objects between storage classes based on how recently they were accessed?
options: Autoclass
Nearline
Coldline
Hyperdisk
questions.explanation: Autoclass auto-tiers objects by last-access time. Nearline and Coldline are themselves storage classes (destinations, not the automation), and Hyperdisk is high-performance block storage, not object storage.

questions.question: By default, Pub/Sub delivers messages with exactly-once semantics, so subscribers never need to handle duplicate deliveries.
questions.explanation: Pub/Sub is at-least-once by default; exactly-once is an opt-in feature (GA on pull subscriptions within one region). Treating Pub/Sub as exactly-once by default is called out as a red flag, so consumers must be idempotent.

questions.question: The Google Cloud Well-Architected Framework reviews every workload against how many pillars, and which of these is one of them?
options: Six pillars; Sustainability is one of them
Five pillars; 'AI and ML' is one of them
Six pillars; 'System design' is one of them
Five pillars; 'Financial services' is one of them
questions.explanation: The framework has six pillars, and Sustainability is one of them. 'AI and ML' and 'Financial services' are cross-pillar perspectives (not pillars), there is no 'System design' pillar, and the count is six, not five.

questions.question: A single-region app must survive the loss of one zone. What is the primary intra-region mechanism, and what does Cloud SQL HA provide?
options: Deploy regionally across multiple zones; Cloud SQL HA keeps a synchronous standby in a second zone with ~60s failover on the same IP
Immediately go multi-region; Cloud SQL HA asynchronously replicates to a second region with zero RPO
Run a single zone with larger machines; Cloud SQL HA snapshots the disk hourly for restore
Use a global anycast LB only; Cloud SQL HA promotes a cross-region read replica automatically
questions.explanation: Multi-zone (regional) deployment is the primary intra-region HA mechanism, and Cloud SQL HA is a regional instance with a synchronous standby in a second zone (~60s failover, same IP). Jumping to multi-region before exhausting multi-zone is a red flag, and HA is zonal/synchronous, not cross-region async.

questions.question: How does Cloud Run's request-based (default) billing model differ from instance-based billing?
options: Request-based bills CPU only during requests plus a per-request fee; instance-based bills CPU for the whole instance lifetime
Request-based bills a flat monthly fee per instance; instance-based bills only per HTTP request
Request-based never scales to zero; instance-based always scales to zero
Request-based bills for memory only; instance-based bills for network egress only
questions.explanation: Request-based billing charges CPU only during request processing plus a per-request fee, while instance-based charges CPU for the entire instance lifetime. The other options invert or fabricate the billing dimensions.

questions.question: In the GCP resource hierarchy (Organization → Folder → Project), how is a project's effective IAM policy determined?
options: It is the union of the project's own bindings plus all bindings inherited from its ancestor folders and the organization
It is only the bindings set directly on the project; ancestor bindings are ignored
It is the intersection of the project's bindings with the organization's bindings
A child folder's bindings override and replace any bindings set at the organization level
questions.explanation: IAM bindings are inherited downward, and a project's effective policy is the union of its own bindings with all ancestors'. It is not an intersection, ancestors are not ignored, and child policies add to (not replace) ancestor grants.

questions.question: Google Cloud Armor provides managed WAF rules and DDoS protection. What specifically requires the Cloud Armor Enterprise tier?
options: Adaptive Protection — ML-based L7 DDoS detection
Attaching the WAF rules to the load balancer backend
The preconfigured OWASP Top 10 rule set
Basic L3/L4 volumetric DDoS protection
questions.explanation: Adaptive Protection (ML-based L7 DDoS detection) is gated to the Cloud Armor Enterprise tier. Attaching WAF rules to the LB, the preconfigured OWASP rules, and L3/L4 DDoS protection are part of the standard offering.

questions.question: You need a wide-column store for very high write throughput and low latency at petabyte scale for time-series/IoT data, with no need for joins or cross-row transactions. Which GCP service fits best?
options: Cloud Bigtable
Firestore
Cloud Spanner
BigQuery
questions.explanation: Cloud Bigtable is the wide-column, petabyte-scale, low-latency store for time-series and high write throughput, with no joins. Firestore is a serverless document DB, Spanner is relational with strong consistency, and BigQuery is an analytics warehouse.

questions.question: Configuring a BigQuery multi-region (e.g. US or EU) automatically provides cross-region replication that protects against a full regional outage.
questions.explanation: A BigQuery multi-region is for higher quotas and data residency — it does NOT provide automatic cross-region replication or regional-outage redundancy. For a total-region outage you need BigQuery managed disaster recovery; treating multi-region as automatic DR is the named trap.

questions.question: When is Cloud Spanner the right choice over Cloud SQL?
options: When you need horizontal write scaling AND strong global consistency with relational semantics — a single-primary DB has outgrown vertical scale
When you need a cheap, simple regional database for a small CRUD app
When you need a serverless document database with real-time mobile listeners
When you need a serverless columnar analytics warehouse for ad-hoc SQL over petabytes
questions.explanation: Spanner is for horizontal write scaling with strong global consistency and relational semantics, when a single-primary DB has outgrown vertical scale. A small regional CRUD app fits Cloud SQL, mobile documents fit Firestore, and analytics fits BigQuery.

questions.question: Select ALL statements that are TRUE about edge and networking on Google Cloud.
options: Cloud CDN and Cloud Armor attach to the load balancer's backend
The Network Load Balancer (L4) is a passthrough option that preserves the client IP and is regional
Media CDN is the option to reach for with high-volume video, separate from Cloud CDN
On GCP, DNS performs the global geo-steering of users to the nearest region
Cloud Armor's WAF should be placed directly on each application instance rather than the load balancer
questions.explanation: Cloud CDN/Cloud Armor attach to the LB backend, the Network LB is a regional L4 passthrough that preserves client IP, and Media CDN handles high-volume video. Claiming DNS does global steering and putting the WAF off the load balancer are both explicit red flags.

questions.question: Select ALL statements that are TRUE about Pub/Sub as described in the reviewer.
options: It is a global serverless pub/sub bus that doubles as the streaming-ingestion layer
Default message retention is 7 days, extendable to a maximum of 31 days, with seek/replay
Exactly-once delivery, when enabled, is GA on pull subscriptions within a single region
Pub/Sub Lite is the recommended go-forward product and is being expanded in 2026
Cloud Tasks is preferred over Pub/Sub when you need per-queue rate limiting and scheduled delivery
questions.explanation: Pub/Sub covers both queue/fan-out and the streaming bus, defaults to 7-day retention (max 31) with seek/replay, and exactly-once is GA on single-region pull subscriptions; Cloud Tasks is the rate-limited/scheduled task-queue choice. Pub/Sub Lite is deprecated and shuts down June 30 2026, so it is not a go-forward product.

questions.question: Why does the reviewer say GCP's global-by-design building blocks reduce — but do not eliminate — disaster-recovery work for a multi-region architecture?
options: The global anycast LB already does automatic cross-region failover for traffic, but the data tier still needs an explicit RPO/RTO decision and customer-owned failover
Every GCP data store automatically replicates and fails over across regions, so DR is fully handled by the platform
The LB cannot fail over across regions, so you must engineer all traffic steering by hand with DNS
Multi-region is cheaper than multi-zone, so the only remaining work is cost optimization
questions.explanation: The global anycast LB handles the traffic side of DR automatically, but the data tier still demands an explicit RPO/RTO choice with customer-owned failover. Not all data stores auto-fail-over (e.g. the BigQuery trap), the LB does fail over across regions, and smaller RPO/RTO costs more.

questions.subtopic: Cloud SQL HA vs read replicas
questions.question: A senior engineer must distinguish Cloud SQL's HA standby from its read replicas. Which statement is correct?
options: The HA standby is a synchronous failover target in a second zone; read replicas are separate instances (same- or cross-region) used to scale reads
The HA standby serves read traffic to scale reads, while read replicas exist only for failover
Read replicas and the HA standby are the same mechanism with two names
The HA standby asynchronously replicates across regions and is the primary way to serve read-heavy traffic
questions.explanation: The HA standby is a synchronous, second-zone failover target (separate from replicas), while read replicas (same- or cross-region) scale reads. The reviewer explicitly notes read replicas are 'separate from the HA standby', so they are not the same mechanism and the standby is not a read-scaling tool.

questions.question: In a governed landing zone, how do Organization Policy Service constraints and IAM policies differ in what they control?
options: Org Policy constraints define what is *allowed* (guardrails); IAM policies define who can do *what* (grants) — and both are inherited down the hierarchy
Org Policy defines who can do what; IAM defines what is allowed on each resource type
Org Policy applies only to the root organization and is never inherited; IAM is set only per project
Org Policy and IAM are interchangeable ways to grant roles to principals
questions.explanation: Org Policy constraints are guardrails on what is allowed; IAM bindings are grants of who can do what — and both are inherited down the hierarchy. The other options swap the two roles, deny inheritance, or wrongly equate guardrails with role grants.

questions.question: A Cloud Run web tier publishes work to Pub/Sub, and Cloud Run worker subscribers process it. Given Pub/Sub's default delivery semantics, what must the worker code guarantee for correctness?
questions.code:
def handle(message):
    order_id = message.attributes["order_id"]
    charge_card(order_id)   # side effect
    message.ack()
options: Idempotency — charge_card must be safe to run more than once, because Pub/Sub is at-least-once and may redeliver the same message
Nothing extra — Pub/Sub guarantees exactly-once by default, so a single charge per message is assured
Strict global ordering — Pub/Sub guarantees total ordering, so duplicates cannot occur
Synchronous acks before processing — calling ack() first prevents any redelivery
questions.explanation: Pub/Sub is at-least-once by default, so the same message can be redelivered; charge_card must be idempotent or it may double-charge. Exactly-once is opt-in (not the default), Pub/Sub does not provide total ordering by default, and acking before the side effect risks losing work, not preventing duplicates.
